did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning algorithms, specifically comparing ten pretrained convolutional neural network (CNN) models (VGG-19, GoogLeNet, InceptionV3, ResNet50V2, ResNet101V2, ResNet152V2, InceptionResNetV2, DenseNet201, MobileNet, and NASNetMobile) to classify images of six hyperpigmented skin diseases (melasma, naevus fusco-caeruleus zygomaticus, freckles, cafe-au-lait spots, nevus of ota, and lentigo simplex). MobileNet was selected as the optimal model for an auxiliary diagnosis system.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the collection of 1366 skin lesion images, the training and comparison of ten specific deep learning models (VGG-19, GoogLeNet, etc.), and the reporting of new quantitative results including accuracy, AUC, and training times. It includes standard research sections such as Methods, Results, and Conclusion, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Image classification and auxiliary diagnosis system for hyperpigmented skin diseases based on deep learning
brief_summary: This study aimed to identify the optimal deep learning algorithm for the auxiliary diagnosis of six common hyperpigmented skin diseases: melasma, naevus fusco-caeruleus zygomaticus, freckles, cafe-au-lait spots, nevus of ota, and lentigo simplex. The researchers collected 1,366 clinical skin lesion images from the Third Xiangya Hospital and evaluated ten pretrained convolutional neural network models, including VGG-19, GoogLeNet, InceptionV3, ResNet variants, DenseNet201, MobileNet, and NASNetMobile. Model performance was assessed using accuracy, area under the curve (AUC), training time, and parameter count on a dataset split into training and test sets in a 4:1 ratio. MobileNet demonstrated the best clinical application prospect, achieving a test set accuracy of 70.39% and an AUC of 0.93, while requiring the shortest training time of 101 minutes among the high-performing models. The study concludes that MobileNet offers high accuracy and robust generalization ability, leading to the development of an online auxiliary diagnosis system for hyperpigmented skin diseases.

all_task_types.target: Six hyperpigmented skin disease categories (melasma, naevus fusco-caeruleus zygomaticus, freckles, cafe-au-lait spots, nevus of ota, and lentigo simplex)
all_task_types.details: Multiclass classification of clinical skin lesion images into six discrete diagnostic categories using ten pretrained convolutional neural network models (VGG-19, GoogLeNet, InceptionV3, ResNet50V2, ResNet101V2, ResNet152V2, InceptionResNetV2, DenseNet201, MobileNet, and NASNetMobile). Performance was evaluated using accuracy and Area Under the Curve (AUC) metrics.

datasets_used.name: Third Xiangya Hospital Hyperpigmented Skin Disease Dataset
datasets_used.description: A private clinical dataset of skin lesion images collected from the Department of Dermatology at the Third Xiangya Hospital of Central South University. Images were diagnosed by two specialized dermatologists, with reflectance confocal microscopy (RCM) used for controversial cases. The data was deposited at an open-source website (https://github.com/AIdental/six-classifications-skin-disease).

use.training: Used to train and compare ten pretrained convolutional neural network models (VGG-19, GoogLeNet, InceptionV3, ResNet50V2, ResNet101V2, ResNet152V2, InceptionResNetV2, DenseNet201, MobileNet, and NASNetMobile).
use.testing: Used to evaluate model performance (accuracy, AUC) and test robustness against noise and low-definition images.
use.other: Used to develop and deploy an online auxiliary diagnosis system.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the degree to which predicted values match true values for training and test sets.
To measure the quality of the deep learning classification models and compare classifier performance.
To visually present the results of algorithm performance and display differences between classification results and actual values.
To evaluate model performance, particularly in the context of ROC curves and robustness testing against noise.
To visualize the trade-off between sensitivity and specificity for model evaluation.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, Area Under the Curve (AUC), training time, and parameter count, without statistical significance testing.

Third Xiangya Hospital Hyperpigmented Sk.split: Training (1089 images) and Test (277 images) in 4:1 ratio
Third Xiangya Hospital Hyperpigmented Sk.experiment: Comparison of 10 pretrained CNN models for 6-class classification of hyperpigmented skin diseases
Third Xiangya Hospital Hyperpigmented Sk.notes: MobileNet was selected as the optimal model due to its balance of high accuracy (70.39% test), high AUC (0.93), low parameter count, and shortest training time (101 min) among high-performing models. It showed good robustness against noise (2%-50%) and low-definition images. MobileNet performed best on Cafe-au-lait spots (CS), followed by Nevus of Ota (NO), Freckles (FC), Naevus fusco-caeruleus zygomaticus (NZ), and Lentigo simplex (LS), with the worst performance on Melasma (ML).

architecture_used.main_model: MobileNet (selected as optimal); compared with VGG-19, GoogLeNet, InceptionV3, ResNet50V2, ResNet101V2, ResNet152V2, InceptionResNetV2, DenseNet201, and NASNetMobile
architecture_used.pretraining: Pretrained models (specific pretraining datasets not explicitly named, but implied ImageNet or similar standard pretrained weights given the model names)

specific_architectures: VGG-19 (19 layers, convolutional, max pooling, fully connected)
InceptionV3 (approx. 48 layers, Inception modules, decomposition, dimensionality reduction)
InceptionResNetV2 (approx. 572 layers, Inception modules with residual connections)
MobileNet (deep separable convolutions, designed for mobile/embedded devices)
architecture_details.mobile_net_details: Uses deep separable convolutions to reduce computational complexity; comprises convolutional layers, deep separable convolutional layers, and fully connected layers.

preprocessing_techniques: Data preprocessing (general step mentioned, but specific techniques beyond cropping not detailed)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: The paper did not use data augmentation during training. The authors explicitly state in the Discussion section that 'Deep network architectures are data-hungry; several augmentation methods in the literature have been applied... Therefore, an appropriate data augmentation can be employed and the performance of the proposed approach can be evaluated after training with expanded image datasets as an extension of this study,' indicating it was not part of the current study's methodology.

conclusions: MobileNet demonstrated the best clinical application prospect for the auxiliary diagnosis of hyperpigmented skin diseases among the ten tested deep learning models.

MobileNet achieved a test set accuracy of 70.39% and an AUC of 0.93, while requiring the shortest training time (101 minutes) and having the fewest parameters (3,235,014) among the high-performing models.
The MobileNet algorithm exhibited high robustness against noise (up to 50%) and low-definition images, with only slight decreases in sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy.

MobileNet performed best on cafe-au-lait spots (CS), followed by nevus of ota (NO), freckles (FC), naevus fusco-caeruleus zygomaticus (NZ), and lentigo simplex (LS), with the worst performance on melasma (ML).
An online auxiliary diagnosis system was successfully developed and deployed using the MobileNet model to improve diagnostic efficiency and reduce misdiagnosis rates.
The study acknowledges that deep learning models are data-hungry and suggests that future work should employ data augmentation and expand image datasets to further evaluate performance.
Future advancements may improve diagnosis by combining deep learning results with multimodal data such as dermoscopy, reflectance confocal microscopy (RCM), and skin pathology.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a single-center dataset from the Third Xiangya Hospital, raising concerns about external validation and potential population bias.
No data augmentation techniques were employed during training, which the authors acknowledge as a limitation given that deep learning models are data-hungry.
The dataset is small (1,366 images total), with class imbalance present (e.g., Lentigo simplex has only 112 images vs. Freckles with 346).
The test set accuracy for the selected optimal model (MobileNet) was relatively low at 70.39%, suggesting limited generalization performance.
The study did not use any denoising techniques in the pre-processing stage, despite noise being a significant factor in image processing.
The comparison was restricted to only 10 deep learning algorithm models, limiting the comprehensiveness of the algorithm selection.
The auxiliary diagnosis system relies on a computer-aided prediction that is subsequently reviewed by a dermatologist, indicating it is an assistive tool rather than an autonomous diagnostic system.
The paper does not report statistical significance testing (p-values) for the performance differences between models.
